japanese: 台湾まぜそば スペシャル

region_of_origin: Japan (Nagoya-origin style, popularised in Taiwan-influenced Japanese ramen culture)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba or Taiwan mazesoba. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0GXM (Noodle dishes) is the closest available category. No specific mazesoba or dry ramen code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for mazesoba or Taiwan mazesoba as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Taiwan mazesoba special as served at a Japanese ramen restaurant, approximately 450 g including noodles, minced pork topping, egg yolk, and condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen noodle dish with meat topping — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for wheat noodles (cooked), ground pork, soy sauce, chilli oil, and egg yolk; cross-referenced with NCC Food and Nutrient Database generic noodle dish category
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) and NCC Food and Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Taiwan mazesoba exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the principal components: cooked wheat noodles (~55% by weight), cooked ground pork (~20%), soy-based tare sauce (~8%), chilli oil (~3%), egg yolk (~5%), and garnishes (~9%). Values represent a plausible per-100g profile for the assembled dish. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and noodle-based dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles (GI ~54–58, medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwan mazesoba. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked wheat noodles (GI range 54–58, medium category). The dish is dominated by thick wheat noodles (~55% by weight), which are the primary glycemic driver. The fat and protein content from minced pork and egg yolk will moderately attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 x 105.75) / 100 ≈ 58.2, rounded to 58.0. Serving carbohydrate = 23.5 g per 100g x 450g serving = 105.75g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based tare sauce and seasoned broth components
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 520 mg sodium per 100g (equating to ~2340 mg per 450g serving), Taiwan mazesoba special substantially exceeds the WHO single-meal sodium target of 600 mg and should be avoided or consumed only occasionally by individuals managing hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index wheat noodles with high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 (medium) and a glycemic load of ~58 per standard bowl, Taiwan mazesoba special will produce a significant postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, saturated fat from minced pork, and cholesterol from egg yolk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~2340 mg per serving), saturated fat (~10.8 g per serving), and dietary cholesterol (~292 mg per serving) from minced pork and raw egg yolk makes Taiwan mazesoba special a dish that individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume infrequently and in reduced portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwan mazesoba special (台湾まぜそば スペシャル) is a Japanese dry noodle dish originating from the Nagoya ramen scene, inspired by Taiwanese minced pork rice (lu rou fan) flavour profiles. Unlike conventional ramen, mazesoba is served completely without broth — thick, chewy wheat noodles are tossed directly with a rich, umami-forward tare sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, chilli oil, and rendered pork fat. The 'special' variant typically includes a generous topping of seasoned minced pork (niku miso), a raw egg yolk placed in the centre, nori sheets, menma (fermented bamboo shoots), green onions, fish powder (niboshi), and additional chilli for mild spice. The diner mixes all components vigorously before eating, coating every strand of noodle in the sauce. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, mildly spicy, and slightly smoky, with a rich mouthfeel from the egg yolk and pork fat. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 198 kcal per 100g, with moderate protein (~9.2g), moderate fat (~7.8g), and significant carbohydrate (~23.5g) from the wheat noodles. Sodium content is high (~520 mg per 100g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (medium), with a high glycemic load per standard serving (~58), relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. In Singapore, Taiwan mazesoba is served at specialist Japanese ramen restaurants such as Ramen Matsuri.
common_user_queries: Is mazesoba the same as ramen?
What is the difference between mazesoba and ramen?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Taiwan mazesoba exists in any approved primary nutritional database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central component entries. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-based estimation methodology is systematic and the primary glycemic driver (wheat noodles) has a well-characterised GI in the approved source.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate. All other -1 fields have been replaced with estimated values from component analysis. Nutrient values represent a per-100g profile for the assembled dish as served (noodles, minced pork, egg yolk, sauce, and garnishes combined). The serving size of 450g is an estimate for a standard restaurant bowl; actual restaurant portions may vary. Sodium content is particularly variable depending on the amount of tare sauce applied. The GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Taiwan mazesoba has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. Glycemic load per serving = (55 x 105.75) / 100 = 58.2, rounded to 58.0.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is canonical English 'Taiwan Mazesoba Special'; Japanese local name 台湾まぜそば スペシャル is present satisfying G6 for Japanese cuisine.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros (energy_kcal 198.0, protein_g 9.2, fat_g 7.8, carbohydrate_g 23.5) are present, numeric, and non-zero. Source is USDA FoodData Central composite estimate, clearly documented in nutrient_source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.2), sodium_mg (520.0), saturated_fat_g (2.4), and sugar_g (1.8) are all present as numeric values.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55, numeric >0), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (58.0), and gi_source are all present. GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle entry, with estimation basis documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish, closest_parent) and FoodEx2 A0GXM (Noodle dishes, closest_parent) are defensible anchors. SNOMED CT is null with gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains preparation, taste, texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases list is comprehensive with 14 entries covering English variants, Japanese script, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language is populated for english, japanese, and romanized. All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list. Consistency check passed.
